title: Not all black holes decohere quantum superpositions
abstract: We study the decoherence induced by near-extremal charged black holes on quantum systems in their exterior. Specifically, we analyze a thought experiment recently discussed in the literature, where the quantum system is a charged particle prepared in a spatial superposition. Near-extremal black holes are known to exhibit large quantum metric fluctuations of the near-horizon geometry at low temperatures. We show that, at late times, if the black hole is sufficiently close to extremality, these quantum gravity effects make the decoherence rate vanish. This phenomenon is due to a spin-induced energy gap in the quantum black hole spectrum. For energies above the gap, the decoherence rate becomes nonzero, but is still suppressed relative to semiclassical expectations, so these quantum gravity effects always enhance the coherence of the superposition.
summary: This paper demonstrates that quantum gravity effects in near-extremal black holes can suppress the decoherence of external quantum systems due to a spin-induced energy gap. It reveals that, unlike the semiclassical prediction, certain black holes can be 'transparent' to quantum superpositions, thereby enhancing coherence.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the decoherence of a quantum system in the exterior of a near-extremal charged black hole is a universal feature or if quantum gravity effects can suppress it.
main_result: The authors show that for near-extremal black holes, a spin-induced energy gap causes the decoherence rate to vanish at late times for certain energy regimes, meaning quantum gravity effects actually enhance the coherence of the external system.
method: The study uses a low-energy effective theory approach, employing gravitational path integrals, the Schwarzian action, and an expansion of the influence functional to calculate the evolution of the reduced density matrix.
model_or_system: The system consists of a single electrically charged particle (a qubit/dipole) in a spatial superposition held stationary outside a near-extremal Reissner-Nordström black hole, with interactions mediated by the electromagnetic field.
key_observables: Decoherence rate (Gamma), absorption cross-section, and the evolution of the reduced density matrix.
important_parameters: Near-extremality, energy scale (Eb), spin-induced energy gap, Hawking temperature, and the low-frequency/late-time limits.
main_assumptions: The particle's separation and recombination are performed slowly enough to neglect radiation emission; the analysis focuses on electromagnetic interactions and the Schwarzian regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the experimental setup; Figure 2 compares semiclassical and Schwarzian-corrected decoherence rates; Figure 3 compares decoherence rates for scalar and electromagnetic fields, highlighting the transparency window for photons.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of black hole-induced decoherence, defines the physical model and effective theory, performs perturbative calculations of the decoherence rate, compares different field types (scalar vs. photon), and validates the quantum results by matching them to the semiclassical limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of the quantum system in the black hole exterior (left) and the relevant scales in the problem (right). Alice’s state is the superposition of an electric dipole pointing in two opposite directions. We consider a low-energy, long-time limit where the black hole can be treated as a point particle in flat space.

Figure 2: Comparison of the semiclassical (B.9) (black) and Schwarzian-corrected (B.14) (red) predictions for the electromagnetic decoherence rate Γ as a function of the black hole energy above extremality E/Eb. On the vertical axis, the decoherence rate is expressed in units of E5 b e2q2d2r8 0b−6

Figure 3: The Schwarzian-corrected decoherence rate Γ as a function of E/Eb. We compare the case where the quantum system sources a scalar field (4.16) (left) and an electromagnetic field (B.14) (right). The energy window (4.12) where the decoherence rate vanishes extends up to E/Eb = 1 for the photon, while it is absent for the scalar. While higher order effects will give small modifications to the non-zero part of the curves, they do not modify the fact that Γ = 0 for E < Eb in the electromagnetic case (cf. (4.19) and (4.21)). The scalar and electromagnetic decoherence rates are expressed in units of Ebj2 Ar2 0b−2 and E5 b e2q2d2r8 0b−6, respectively. As in Figure 2, one should not focus...

Figure 4: Schwarzian-corrected decoherence rate of the ℓ= 0 scalar (4.16) (green) vs. ℓ= 1 photon (B.14) (red) as a function of E/Eb. Note that the electromagnetic decoherence rate surpasses the scalar one for E ≳5Eb.
